text: In this video, we present a case of robotic transabdominal interspecteric resection and stapled colloenal anastomosis with the DaVinci XI robotic system. A thirty-one years old gentleman presented to us with complaints of bleeding perectum. He was diagnosed to have a non-metastatic low rectal adenocarcinoma. MRI scan showed a low rectal growth, five centimeters from the anal verge, with multiple enlarged mesorectal lymph nodes. He received long-course concurrent chemoradiotherapy. Post CT-RT MRI scan showed partial response and he was planned for a robotic low anterior resection.
The robotic cart is docked to the patient, with the robotic arms positioned optimally for organ-specific targeting, using the standard port configuration.

The surgeon begins dissection medial to the duodenal jejunal flexure, lifting the inferior mesenteric vein anteriorly and dissecting posteriorly in the avascular plane. The inferior mesenteric vein is clipped and divided. The pancreatic attachment of the mesocolon is divided up to the pancreas tail to facilitate complete splenic flexure mobilization. The mesocolon is dissected off the Gerota's fascia inferiorly. A medial to lateral dissection is initiated by incising the medial peritoneal fold, continuing in the avascular plane while lifting the mesocolon off retroperitoneal structures, safeguarding the ureter and gonadal vessels. Lymph node dissection is performed at the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, followed by high ligation of the IMA. The dissection proceeds laterally to ease mobilization of the descending colon from the lateral peritoneal fold. The sigmoid colon is retracted medially, and the lateral peritoneal fold is incised superiorly up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon incises the splenocolic ligament and enters the lesser sac to achieve complete splenic flexure mobilization. The rectum is retracted anteriorly, and dissection begins in the posterior extramesorectal plane, utilizing traction and counter traction for precise tissue separation. The robotic system's seven degrees of motion enhance dexterity, aiding the dissection deep in the pelvis.
Using the Da Vinci XI robotic system, the surgeon incises the lateral and anterior peritoneal folds after completing sufficient posterior mobilization within the pelvic region.

The surgeon carefully dissects the rectal tube away from the seminal vesicles and vas deferens, incising the Denonvilliers fascia to proceed inferiorly in the anterior plane. For bulky anterior tumors, the fascia is retained on the specimen side during dissection. Posterolateral dissection is performed as low as possible to expose the levator muscles on both sides.

The junction of the levators in the midline is clearly visible. The surgeon uses the X-eye system to enhance the field of vision deep in the pelvis, facilitating posterior dissection. Partial intraspinctric dissection is performed to achieve an adequate distal margin.

The surgeon transects the rectal tube distally using an Eichelon-Sixty endoscopic linear stapler. A 5 cm transverse incision is made in the left iliac fossa to extract the specimen. The anvil of a circular stapler is placed in the descending colon and repositioned in the abdominal cavity. A 29 mm circular stapler is inserted into the anal canal, and the anvil is articulated with the trocar. The stapler is approximated and fired, creating a stapled coloanal anastomosis.

A temporary covering ileostomy was done. The total operative time was four hours and the estimated blood loss was fifty ml. The patient was discharged on post-operative day five without any significant morbidity.

Histopathology showed YPT-N-B residual viable adenocarcinoma. The distal resection margin was two centimeters and free of tumor. The TMA quality was complete and the circumferential resection margin was free.
